title: Caribbean black cake
subtitle: Caribbean rum-soaked fruit cake
excerpt: Raisins, currants, sultanas, prunes, and cherries soaked in rum and wine for weeks and then blended to a paste, folded into a dark, spiced batter made almost black by browning sauce and molasses. Black cake is the cake of Caribbean celebration — cut at Christmas, weddings, and christenings, a dense, spirit-heavy, long-keeping fruit cake that is entirely unlike a British Christmas cake.
sourceNotes: Caribbean black cake has direct roots in the British tradition of heavily fruited dark cakes brought to the islands by colonisers, transformed by local ingredients — browning sauce, rum, and the regional preference for much more fruit than European versions. The practice of grinding the soaked fruit (rather than leaving it whole as in British Christmas cake) is a distinctly Caribbean technique. Black cake appears across the Caribbean with regional variations in Jamaica, Trinidad, Barbados, and Guyana.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes very well. Keeps for 3 months at room temperature when wrapped tightly. Continues to mature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The fruit must be soaked in rum for at least 1 week; 1 month is ideal and up to 1 year is common. The longer the soak, the richer the cake.

content.text: The fruit preparation is the most important step. Combine the dried fruits in a jar, cover with dark rum and a splash of red wine, and seal. Leave for as long as possible — a minimum of 1 week, ideally 1 month, traditionally 1 year. When ready to bake, drain the fruit and reserve the soaking liquid, then grind the fruit to a paste in a food processor. The ground paste, not whole fruit, goes into the batter.

The batter will be very dark — almost black from the browning sauce and molasses. This is correct. The finished cake should be so dark it is nearly black on the inside. After baking, the cake is doused in more rum while hot. This soaking step is not optional; it keeps the cake moist and allows it to mature for weeks or months.

items.prepNote: for soaking fruit, plus extra for dousing

content.text: At least 1 week before baking: combine the {{raisins}}, {{currants}}, {{sultanas}}, {{prunes}}, and {{cherries-glace}} in a large jar. Pour over the {{rum-dark}} and seal. Leave in a dark cupboard, shaking occasionally. When ready to bake: drain the fruit (reserve the rum) and process in a food processor to a rough paste.

Heat the oven to 150°C. Line a 23 cm round tin (or two 20 cm tins) with a double layer of greaseproof paper. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{light-brown-sugar}} together until pale. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{nutmeg}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{browning-sauce}}, {{molasses}}, and {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the fruit paste and enough of the reserved soaking rum to give a dropping consistency (about 3 tablespoons). The batter will be almost black. Pour into the tin and bake for 1 hour 45 minutes to 2 hours, until a skewer comes out clean. While still hot, pour 3 to 4 tablespoons of {{rum-dark}} over the top. Wrap tightly when cool.

items.cause: Insufficient soaking rum after baking or underdone fruit soak
items.fix: The post-bake rum dousing is essential for moisture and maturing. Add more rum to the hot cake. The fruit soak time also affects moisture: short-soaked fruit produces a drier, less complex cake
Increase the browning sauce to 3 tablespoons. The cake should be almost black when baked — a pale result means the colouring agents were underused
Bake at 150°C and test with a skewer at 1 hour 45 minutes. A heavy fruit cake retains moisture; allow a full 2 hours if needed

content.text: Caribbean black cake is the celebration cake of the Caribbean diaspora. It is cut at Christmas, at weddings (as the wedding cake, often multi-tiered and iced white over the black interior), and at christenings. Making black cake is an annual event in Caribbean households — the fruit is put to soak in November for the Christmas cake. In Britain the making and gifting of black cake is one of the strongest culinary continuities of the Caribbean diaspora: a practice that connects each generation to the islands, to the families who made it before them, and to the specific smell of rum-soaked fruit that says Christmas.
